verdict_reasoning: TGOLN2 is a type I integral membrane protein with a TM domain, canonically resident in the trans-Golgi network (TGN) and early endosomes. It cycles between these endosomal and trans-Golgi compartments via documented vesicular trafficking. Although the protein's baseline localization is intracellular (endomembrane system), it is a stable member of the secretory vesicle machinery and reaches the plasma membrane transiently during its normal endosomal-recycling cycle. The protein has its own membrane anchor (type I TM) and participates in regulated vesicular trafficking; these recycling dynamics qualify it for contextual surface accessibility via dual_localization. No baseline stable surface pool or tissue-restricted display is documented, but the cycling behavior between early endosomes and TGN places it in active ER-PM junctional and secretory-recycling trafficking.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The extent and residence time of TGOLN2 on the PM during its documented endosomal-TGN recycling cycle are not explicitly quantified in standard references. Whether this transient vesicular-trafficking PM exposure is robust enough to support binding by extracellular therapeutics remains uncertain; most surface-accessibility claims against TGN residents require direct surface-proteomics or cell-surface-biotinylation evidence for this specific protein, which is not provided in the task context.
